Deafblindness inventions

The world becomes touch.GLOVE: Guided Language Over Vibrational Encoding

GLOVE is an AI tactile communication ecosystem for deafblind people. Voice, text, photos, camera scenes, writing and objects are translated into safe tactile language through the smart glove. The user can respond through signs, taps, gestures, pen writing or a tactile keyboard, and the AI voice responder speaks the message aloud.

World to userSpeech, text, photos and scenes become touch patterns.
User to worldSigns, taps, writing and typing become voice or text.
Grows with lifeStarts with simple meaning and expands into conversation.

Immediate visual proof

A full product ecosystem, not one isolated glove.

These visuals explain the invention quickly: a person uses the glasses, glove and voice responder; the product family then extends into the app, smart pen, tactile keyboard, photo translation and charging hub.

How the glove works

The glove is both the receiver and the voice of the user.

The AI Smart Glove is not a normal glove. It is a tactile communication interface. It delivers meaning to the hand and reads the hand when the user answers.

1

The world is captured

Speech, text, photos, camera scenes, writing, object tags and typed messages enter the system.

2

AI simplifies meaning

The AI removes unnecessary detail and turns information into useful concepts, such as question + want + drink.

3

Tactile language is generated

Meaning becomes a pattern built from hand location, rhythm, intensity, pressure, duration and sequence.

4

The user feels the message

Haptic actuators deliver gentle, repeatable touch patterns on the palm, fingers, thumb and wrist.

5

The user responds

The glove reads signs, taps, pressure, gestures, writing or typing. The AI voice responder speaks the answer aloud.

Two-way communication

World to user. User back to world.

Receiving: someone speaks, types, shows a photo or walks into the camera view. The AI understands the important meaning and sends it to the glove as tactile language.

Responding: the user signs, taps, writes or types. The glove detects the response, the AI converts it into normal language, and the voice responder says it out loud.

  • Parent says: “Do you want more food?” → glove sends question + want + more + food.
  • User taps yes: the voice responder says, “Yes.”
  • User signs pain: the voice responder says, “I am in pain.”
  • User writes water: the system can say, “I want water.”

Tactile language

Safe tactile language, not random buzzing.

The core invention is the language system. A deafblind user should not receive confusing vibration noise. The glove must create a consistent touch code that can be learned, repeated and expanded.

Each tactile message can be built from where the touch happens, how fast it pulses, how strong it feels, how long it lasts and what sequence it follows.

ThumbPeople, self, yes/no and identity cues.
Index fingerObjects, names and things the user can touch.
Middle fingerActions such as eat, drink, walk, write or play.
Ring fingerFeelings and body needs such as pain, tired, scared, happy.
Little fingerQuestions, choices, more, finished and confirmation.
Wrist and palmCore words, warnings, routines, transitions and urgent alerts.

Learning from birth

A deafblind baby learns meaning through real objects, real touch and repetition.

The system should begin gently. It should support family connection, specialist teaching and object-based learning. The goal is not to overwhelm the child with full sentences. The goal is to build meaning.

Stage 1

Comfort and trust

The baby becomes comfortable with safe, gentle touch. The system must never replace human bonding.

Stage 2

Cause and effect

Food pattern happens before eating. Drink pattern happens before drinking. The child learns that touch signals mean something.

Stage 3

Object connection

A teacher places a cup in the child’s hand, says “cup,” and the glove sends the cup pattern. Object + experience + touch = meaning.

Stage 4

Choice and expression

The child learns more, stop, yes, no, help, pain, toilet, happy and scared — practical words that create control.

The first vocabulary should be small: mum, dad, food, drink, more, stop, yes, no, help, pain, toilet, sleep, happy, scared, play and finished. The language grows only when the user is ready.

Every invention in the ecosystem

Each product solves one part of communication. Together, they build a daily-life system.

Click any product image to enlarge it. The website text gives the clean explanation; the visuals help investors, families and partners understand the complete product direction quickly.

GLOVE invention

AI Smart Glove

The hand-based communication device. It sends tactile language to the user and reads signs, taps, pressure and gestures from the user.

  • Haptic actuators deliver safe touch patterns.
  • Touch and motion sensors read the user’s response.
  • Patterns can represent people, objects, actions, feelings, questions and warnings.

GLOVE invention

AI Camera Glasses

The world-reader. They detect useful visual information and send simplified meaning to the glove.

  • Recognises text, objects, signs, scenes, photos and hazards.
  • Reduces overwhelming detail into usable meaning.
  • Helps the user ask for more detail when needed.

GLOVE invention

Mobile App / AI Brain

The control centre for translation, vocabulary, learning levels, photos, signs, voice and settings.

  • Turns voice, text, photos and camera scenes into tactile language.
  • Controls baby, child, learning, conversation and emergency modes.
  • Lets families and teachers personalise vocabulary safely.

GLOVE invention

Charging / Connection Hub

A premium home base that charges, connects, protects and synchronises the system.

  • Keeps the glove, glasses, pen, keyboard and responder ready.
  • Useful for home, school, care settings and travel.
  • Designed to make the ecosystem feel simple, not scattered.

GLOVE invention

Smart Pen

A writing and learning tool for letters, tracing, spelling and expression.

  • Detects writing movement and turns it into meaning.
  • Confirms letters and words through the glove.
  • Can help the user write a message that the voice responder speaks aloud.

GLOVE invention

Tactile Keyboard

A typing route for learning, longer messages, education and independent communication.

  • Raised tactile keys support touch-based typing.
  • Typed words can be spoken aloud or sent as text.
  • The glove confirms input so the user knows what was entered.

GLOVE invention

Photo-to-Tactile Translation

A way to turn images into touch-based meaning.

  • Photos become key meaning, not a confusing flood of detail.
  • Examples: dog + animal + sitting, family + happy, warning + wet floor.
  • Useful for memories, learning, safety, classrooms and daily life.

Why these inventions matter

The benefit is communication, confidence, safety and connection.

GLOVE is designed around dignity. It should help deafblind users receive information, make choices, express needs and build communication in a way that can grow with them.

Early meaning

Creates a touch pathway into language for people who cannot rely on sight or sound.

Less guessing

Parents, carers and teachers get clearer yes/no, more, stop, help and pain communication.

Two-way voice

The user’s signs, taps, writing and typing can become spoken words other people understand.

Photo access

Photos, signs, labels and scenes can be translated into tactile meaning.

Education support

Letters, objects, routines, spelling, writing and classroom instructions can connect through touch.

Safer movement

Camera glasses can identify hazards, doors, steps, people and objects before sending useful alerts.

Personalised growth

The system can begin with core words and expand into phrases, questions and conversation.

Real-world dignity

The goal is to support independence without replacing human touch, family connection or specialist teaching.

Day-to-day life

What changes during an ordinary day?

  • Morning: the glove signals food, drink, clothes, bathroom and next routine.
  • Learning: a real object is placed in the hand while the glove sends the matching tactile word.
  • Outside: glasses detect steps, doors, people, traffic, signs or hazards and send simple alerts.
  • Photos: family pictures, dog pictures and warning signs become touch-based meaning.
  • Communication: the user signs, taps, writes or types; the AI voice responder speaks the answer.

Prototype roadmap

Start with the smallest test that proves tactile language can be learned.

  • Prototype 1: soft glove or hand pad with 8–12 haptic points and 20 core words.
  • Prototype 2: app-based pattern editor, training mode and safe intensity control.
  • Prototype 3: speech-to-simple-meaning and text-to-tactile translation.
  • Prototype 4: tap/sign response translated into voice output.
  • Prototype 5: review with deafblind specialists, families, educators, engineers and safeguarding experts.

Responsible design

Built to support human communication, not replace it.

This invention concept needs professional testing, ethics review, sensory safety review and deafblind education input before any use with babies or children.

  • Comfort mode, quiet mode and user-controlled pause.
  • Safe materials, safe intensity and easy cleaning.
  • Privacy-first handling of voice, camera and learning data.
  • Compatibility with existing tactile signing, object cues and specialist support.

Inventor concept by Cornelius Aurelius

GLOVE is designed to open another doorway into communication.

A deafblindness invention ecosystem: world to touch, user back to voice.
